method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine volcano in the southwest Pacific Ocean, located just south of Vangunu Island in the western Solomon Islands.[2][3]  
It lies about 24 km south of Vangunu Island and forms part of the Solomon Islands volcanic chain.[2][4]  
The volcano is situated in a highly active tectonic setting on the Pacific Ring of Fire, at a convergent boundary where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate.[1][2]  
Kavachi rises from the seafloor as a roughly conical volcanic edifice from depths of more than 1,000 m.[4][5]  
Its base is about 8 km in diameter, and its shallow summit commonly lies only a few tens of metres below sea level.[2][4]  
Bathymetric observations describe the main summit as an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring approximately 120 m by 75 m, with a rim at an average depth of about 24 m.[5]  
The deepest soundings on the main peak are about 70 m below sea level, while the flanks descend with slopes of roughly 18° to depths greater than 1,000 m.[5]  
A secondary summit, described as a southwest extension, lies about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit and is separated from it by a narrow col.[5]  
Because the summit is so shallow, eruptions can disturb the sea surface, produce discoloured water plumes visible from aircraft or satellites, and occasionally build short-lived islands above sea level.[2][3][4]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is among the most active submarine volcanoes in the Pacific, with near-surface eruptions occurring every few years since its first historically recorded eruption in 1939.[1] Since 1939, eruptive activity has built temporary islands at least nine times, but these islands have generally been too small and poorly armoured by solid lava to resist wave erosion and have usually disappeared within months.[1] In late 1951, the volcano was associated with frequent small tsunamis, with one source giving a possible date of 22 December 1951 for the activity.[1]

A renewed island-building phase was observed on 14 May 2000 by scientists aboard the CSIRO research vessel ''Franklin'', when eruptions ejected ash and incandescent lava blocks up to 70 m above sea level and produced sulfurous steam plumes rising to about 500 m.[2] At that time, Kavachi had reportedly been dormant for nine years, and its summit was forming a sandy, ashy beach about 2 m below sea level.[2] Explosive eruptions occurred daily from 27 November to 13 December 2001, and by 13 January 2002 volcanic debris and gas bubbles were observed rising from the submarine vent, accompanied by frequent loud noises.[2] During an eruptive cycle in 2003, a 15 m-high island formed, but by 16 November the summit had been reduced to about 32 m below sea level and no evidence of volcanic activity was observed.[2] Volcanic activity was again reported on 15 March 2004 after a period of quiescence.[2]

In January 2015, an expedition reached Kavachi during a rare lull in activity, allowing observations inside the active crater and along its flanks that are normally prevented by the volcano’s explosive behavior.[3] The expedition documented hydrothermal activity at the summit, including warm-water and gas discharge, and identified a second peak southwest of the main summit with evidence of diffuse-flow venting.[3] The same expedition found biological communities, including gelatinous animals, small fish, and sharks, living inside the active crater, indicating that periods between eruptions can support complex ecosystems in the volcano’s extreme environment.[3]

The Global Volcanism Program has counted 33 historical eruptions at Kavachi, with activity spanning 44 of the 87 years since 1939, implying activity roughly every two years on average.[1] This frequency is probably an underestimate because older and remote submarine eruptions may have gone unobserved.[1] The most recent eruptive period began in 2021 and was reported as continuing as of May 2026.[1]

==Marine life==
Marine life has been observed in Kavachi's crater during quiescent periods, despite warm, acidic and mineral-rich waters affected by volcanic gases.[2][4] A reported expedition documented an ecosystem that included gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish and sharks within the crater environment.[4] The shark species identified included the scalloped hammerhead shark (*Sphyrna lewini*) and the silky shark (*Carcharhinus falciformis*), both filmed swimming inside the crater.[4] Camera observations also recorded the sharks showing aggressive behaviour toward bait deployed by researchers.[4]

Environmental measurements reported for the crater included water temperatures up to 13 °C above surrounding conditions, reaching about 42 °C, and an estimated pH of about 6.1.[4] The hydrothermal plume contained suspended particles and gases rich in carbon dioxide and sulfur, creating conditions suitable for microbial communities.[4] Bacteria of the genus *Sulfurimonas*, which can use sulfur and carbon dioxide in chemosynthesis, have been reported from the plume environment.[4]

The presence of large predators in such conditions has raised questions about the physiological and behavioural limits of marine animals.[4] Proposed explanations include sharks entering during calm phases, detecting chemical or seismic warning signs before eruptions, or tolerating elevated temperature and acidity.[1] It remains unclear whether sharks reside in the crater permanently or move through it periodically, and long-term observations have been proposed to clarify their behaviour across eruptive cycles.[5] The crater ecosystem has therefore been described as a natural laboratory for studying marine resilience, adaptation to extreme environments and the interaction between volcanism and biology.[1][5]
